title: Equilibrium and dynamics of a three-state opinion model on a network of networks
authors: Irene Ferri, Albert Díaz-Guilera, Hiroki Sayama
abstract: Opinion formation models typically represent each individual as a single variable. However, in practice each individual holds interconnected beliefs whose internal organization may influence collective outcomes. To explore this dependence, we study a three-state opinion model on a network of networks in which each agent has an internal belief graph and interacts with other agents through an external social graph. Each belief can take two opposite polarized states or a neutral one and a neutrality parameter tunes the relative conviction of the neutral stance. We incorporate temperature into the model to account for external social agitation and for the tolerance of internal cognitive dissonance. We explore the stationary state and dynamics of the model using analytical approaches and Monte Carlo simulations on a fully connected external social graph, with internal belief topologies given by one-dimensional chains, cliques, and star-like structures, where there is a central core belief to which all other beliefs are connected. We find that the critical temperature at which the polarized consensus destabilizes increases with the addition of more beliefs to star-like agents but saturates in the case of ring- and clique-like internal topologies. We also consider binary mixtures of agents with different internal topologies in equal proportions, showing that the interplay between agents is regime-dependent, with the dominant topology depending on the value of the neutrality parameter.
summary: This paper explores a multi-scale opinion model where agents possess their own internal networks of beliefs. It demonstrates that the internal arrangement of these beliefs (e.g., star vs. ring) fundamentally changes how social consensus or polarization emerges at different temperatures and neutrality levels.
main_problem: Investigating how the internal topology of an individual's belief system (the organization of interconnected beliefs) influences collective opinion formation, social consensus, and phase transitions in a network of networks.
main_result: The critical temperature for consensus destabilization increases with the number of beliefs in star-like agents but saturates in ring and clique topologies; additionally, mixing different agent topologies leads to regime-dependent dominance where rings act as catalysts for extremist convergence.
method: The study employs Mean-Field Approximation (MFA) to analytically derive order parameters and free energy, complemented by Metropolis Monte Carlo simulations to study zero-temperature dynamics and finite-temperature phase transitions.
model_or_system: A three-state (positive, negative, neutral) opinion model on a multilayer graph where each agent has an internal belief graph (clique, ring, or star) and interacts with others via an external social graph. The Hamiltonian includes terms for social homophily and internal belief coherence, incorporating temperature to represent social agitation and cognitive dissonance.
key_observables: Net polarization (magnetization m), neutral fraction (n0), internal magnetization (M_int), internal energy (E_int), and critical temperature (Tc).
important_parameters: Neutrality parameter (alpha) controlling the weight of the neutral state, temperature (T) representing social agitation/dissonance, and the number of internal beliefs (c).
main_assumptions: The external social network is assumed to be a fixed, fully connected graph; for star-like agents, the identity of the central hub belief is assumed to be the same across all agents.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes agent belief topologies; Figure 2 shows the three internal network structures; Figure 3 illustrates energy contributions; Figure 4 shows order parameters vs. temperature; Figure 6 compares magnetization curves between MC and MFA; Figure 10 shows magnetization and neutrality distributions in mixed populations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the network-of-networks model, defines the Hamiltonian and topologies, presents analytical Mean-Field results, validates them with Monte Carlo simulations, explores the effects of increasing belief count, analyzes zero-temperature dynamics/steady states, and concludes with the study of mixed-topology populations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Illustration of the three network topologies used to model belief systems with a number of internal beliefs c = 10.
FIG. 3. Contribution of a pair of interacting agents to the energy of the system.

FIG. 4. Magnetization curves for a particular belief as a function of temperature for each internal topology and different numbers of internal beliefs. Results obtained with MC simulations for a system of N = 104 agents, neutrality parameter α = 0, averaged over 100 repetitions with c · 104 MC steps. Comparisons with MFA results are shown for the clique and the ring.

FIG. 5. Critical temperature versus number of beliefs when α = 0 for the clique (blue circles), the ring (red crosses), and the star-like (green dots) agent types. Results obtained averaging 100 repetitions with systems of N = 104 agents.
